title: The Usage of Music in Evangelion
display_title: The Usage of Music in Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: The Usage of Music in Evangelion             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:09 am Music tangent split from here. Allemann wrote: Even if given true, Anno is ignorant about Western art besides pop-culture phenomena like Kubrick's Odyssey and science-fiction pulps.

[]: Re: Sure is convenient here             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:14 am Xard wrote: Well, he is a classic music fag though

[]: Re: Sure is convenient here             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:17 am Allemann wrote: Händel's Messiah and the fourth movement of Beethoven's Ninth are constantly heard in commercials and movies.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:27 am An experienced classical music listener wouldn't use it so awkwardly like Anno did, specially not the one with a chorus.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:28 am Allemann wrote: An experienced classical music listener wouldn't use it so awkwardly like Anno did, specially not the one with a chorus.

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:34 am Xard wrote: Use what? Messiah? Ode to Joy? Polovetsian Dances?

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 8:36 am Allemann wrote: Messiah and the Ode to Joy. I haven't watched 2.0, so I don't now what he did there.

[]:             Quote Post by ran1 » Mon Oct 11, 2010 8:50 am This thread makes me want to masturbate furiously -- intellectually of course.

[]:             Quote Post by Uriel Septim VII » Mon Oct 11, 2010 4:35 pm It can't be that Anno used what seem to be among the top ten most overused classical pieces (Hallelujah, Cello Suite, Air, 9th) and only those pieces by coincidence or because he lacks knowledge of classical music beyond the most commonly shared reference pool. There must be a reason for his shunting (comparatively) obscure pieces.

[]:             Quote Post by Chrad » Mon Oct 11, 2010 6:37 pm Uriel Septim VII wrote: It can't be that Anno used what seem to be among the top ten most overused classical pieces (Hallelujah, Cello Suite, Air, 9th) and only those pieces by coincidence or because he lacks knowledge of classical music beyond the most commonly shared reference pool.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 11, 2010 10:00 pm Allemann wrote: An experienced classical music listener wouldn't use it so awkwardly like Anno did, specially not the one with a chorus.

[]:             Quote Post by Uriel Septim VII » Mon Oct 11, 2010 10:28 pm What is exactly an "experienced classical music listener", and what bars them from filmmaking?

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Mon Oct 11, 2010 10:34 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Errr, WTF? Experienced classical music listeners don't make films and anime, and what makes you think their usages are awkward? Apparently you've never heard of the concept of counterpoint in film music?

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 11, 2010 10:34 pm Uriel Septim VII wrote: What is exactly an "experienced classical music listener", and what bars them from filmmaking?

[]:             Quote Post by Uriel Septim VII » Mon Oct 11, 2010 10:39 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Nothing bars them from film-making, I was just saying that there's hardly a correlation between listening to classical music and knowing how to use it in films. Even if a film-maker was an experienced classical music listener, that has very little to do with their film-making skills or even how they use music.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Oct 11, 2010 10:47 pm Uriel Septim VII wrote: I'm sure there are filmmakers who know how to use the music because they listen to it a lot, just as there are those who don't necessarily listen to it, but have a natural inclination as to what works and doesn't.

[]:             Quote Post by The Abhorrent » Mon Oct 11, 2010 11:04 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Also, what films are you thinking of that feature "end of the world" scenarios played out to songs like What a Wonderful World? I certainly can't think of any off the top of my head...

[]:             Quote Post by Allemann » Tue Oct 12, 2010 1:03 am Uriel Septim VII wrote: There must be a reason for his shunting (comparatively) obscure pieces.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Oct 12, 2010 1:23 am The Abhorrent wrote: But for clear example (if using a different song), the final scene from Dr. Strangelove (1964) . Interestingly enough, a Stanley Kubrick film.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Tue Oct 12, 2010 10:13 am This reminds me of a thread, long since culled, I fear, from the really old days on AnimeNation. A poster named Naoko-chan vehemently objected to the very counterpoint or dissonance discussed here, claiming that several of her favorite pieces had been permanently "ruined" for her by the association with violent imagery. She particularly objected to the use of Air , and demanded to know whether anyone thought it would have worked had the Normandy invasion from Saving Private Ryan been set to Air . When several of us replied "yes", she accused us of trolling her, and the whole thing broke down into a flame war. Shin-seiki brought up Kubrick and A Clockwork Orange in that thread, though the sequence he had in mind was the brawl against the rival gang set to the goofy strains of Rossini's The Thieving Magpie overture.
summary:
[]: The Usage of Music in Evangelion             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:09 am Music tangent split from here. Allemann wrote: Even if given true, Anno is ignorant about Western art besides pop-culture phenomena like Kubrick's Odyssey and science-fiction pulps.

[]: Re: Sure is convenient here             Quote Post by Allemann » Mon Oct 11, 2010 7:14 am Xard wrote:
